_comment: Curated seed for tools/New-OccupationsWorklist.ps1. THIS is the hand part - a trade read in a
record and attributed to a person. The script does the mechanical part: checks the live GEDCOM,
ticks off anything now recorded, and scans the journal and stories for trades not yet on the list.
Add an item when you find a trade in a record that is not yet an Occupation fact on Ancestry.
'match' is what the script looks for in that person's recorded occupations to call it done -
keep it a short distinctive stem (regex, case-insensitive).

add.name: Thomas Champion
add.group: The three "doorways" - where a trade is nearly all we have
add.evidence: The 1889 marriage register. One line, and the only thing it says about him.

add.name: John Discombe
add.group: The three "doorways" - where a trade is nearly all we have
add.evidence: His daughter's wedding line - "John Discombe, bootmaker". No dates, no birthplace, nothing else.

add.name: William Constantine
add.group: The three "doorways" - where a trade is nearly all we have
add.evidence: Hannah Mills's marriage lines, 2 Aug 1902, Leeds St John - "William Constantine (deceased), POLICEMAN".

add.name: Henry Sturdy Whitehead
add.group: The Whitehead wood trades - three generations, straight down the line
add.evidence: Probate, 10 Apr 1893 - "formerly wood sawyer". Currently recorded only as "Woodman".

add.name: Alfred Whitehead
add.group: The Whitehead wood trades - three generations, straight down the line
add.evidence: The same probate - "to Alfred Whitehead joiner". Henry sawed the wood; Alfred joined it; Henry jr was a wheelwright (already recorded).

add.name: Thomas Ingleby
add.evidence: 1901 census, Eastrington, with Emma and young Arthur. Currently only Scholar / Railway Worker / Goods Porter.

add.name: John Mills
add.evidence: His daughter Hannah's marriage lines. A dray is a horse-drawn cart - he is a railway porter in the censuses and a drayman on the wedding line.

add.name: Thomas Ingleby
add.evidence: Kirkby Malzeard baptism - "Cordwainer, of Mickley". NB: this is the SHOEMAKER Thomas (b. ~1812), not the horseman.

add.name: John Blagg
add.evidence: Written up in story-up-the-midland-line.md - he guarded the goods yards.

add.name: Arthur Ingleby
add.evidence: Royal Navy service record, TNA ADM 188/1117, SS 111246. Currently recorded only as "Boilermaker".

add.name: James R. Best
add.evidence: 1871 census, Horton/Bradford (7619:26022846) - "Robert Best, Head, 32, Bricklayer / Mason, born Tadcaster". He is recorded as ROBERT in 1871 and as JAMES R. in 1881 (7572:24491886, "James R. 43, b. Tadcaster") - same man, same birthplace, ages ten years apart. James ROBERT Best, who went by Robert. His 1881 census note already carries "Bricklayer"; the 1871 entry is what adds mason.

add.name: Jane Farrar
add.evidence: 1871 census, Horton/Bradford - "Jane Best, Wife, 28, Woollen Weaver, born Wibsey". She is Ann Elizabeth's mother and James R. Best's wife (married 30 May 1868, Bradford Cathedral). Brings Bradford's worsted trade into the family.
add.warn: Her age is inconsistent between censuses - 28 in 1871 (b.~1843) but 49 in 1881 (b.~1832), an 11-year gap. The tree carries abt 1832. Not resolved; do not treat either as proven.

ruledOut: "groom" - appears a dozen times in the journal, every one of them **bride**groom in a marriage record. Not the stable trade.
"shepherd" - the only hit is the surname **Shepherdson** (Thomas Shepherdson, the householder young Henry Whitehead boarded with). Not a trade.

"Baister" (Lily Constantine, 1921) - NOT AN ERROR, leave it exactly as it stands. Settled by the 1921 index, which carries her EMPLOYER: **Burton & Burton Clothiers, Byron St Mills** (employer code 341), with occupation code **404/6**, at 55 Monkton St, Hunslet, aged 16. She worked for a Leeds clothing manufacturer, so the basting hand is precisely the job - a baister tacks a garment together before it is machined. "baister" is a real trade word and is already in this file's own vocabulary list. Caveat recorded honestly: I could NOT read the census image - the 1921 viewer (588-page filmstrip) defeats the screenshot tool, which is a different failure from the fresh-tab fix. But the employer field settles the question better than handwriting would, because the doubt was whether the WORD is a trade, not what the enumerator's pen looked like.
